title: Secondary Osteoporosis in a 33-Year-Old Woman — Vimla's Case
subtitle: A Resident Training Module: identifying the cause when early menopause and thyroid dysfunction are present but stimulated cortisol is normal — POI work-up, thyroid–bone axis, exclusion algorithm, and treatment sequencing
authors: Resident training module — illustrative teaching case

relatedChapters.title: Primary Hyperparathyroidism in a Young Adult
Low BMI Young Adult — Multisystem Workup

patient.chiefComplaint: Low bone mineral density identified on DXA — evaluation for secondary osteoporosis.
pmh: Early menopause (premature ovarian insufficiency — age at menopause to be confirmed)
Thyroid dysfunction (on treatment or under evaluation — characterise TSH/free T4)
Normal stimulated cortisol (short Synacthen or equivalent — Cushing syndrome excluded)
No fragility fracture history documented yet — confirm
medications: Levothyroxine if hypothyroid (dose and TSH target to review)
Other medications to audit — glucocorticoids, PPIs, antiepileptics, heparin, GnRH agonists

caseDescription: Vimla, 33 years old, presents with secondary osteoporosis on DXA scanning. She has a history of early menopause and thyroid dysfunction. Stimulated cortisol testing is normal, excluding Cushing syndrome as the driver. The core diagnostic task is to identify which contributor(s) explain her bone loss — estrogen deficiency from premature ovarian insufficiency (POI) is the leading hypothesis, but thyroid status (hyperthyroidism or over-replaced hypothyroidism), hyperparathyroidism, celiac disease, malabsorption, myeloma, and medication effects must be systematically excluded before attributing osteoporosis to a single cause. Treatment must address the underlying disorder and bone-specific therapy together.
keyFindings: 33-year-old female — osteoporosis at this age is always secondary until proven otherwise
Early menopause → estrogen deficiency → accelerated trabecular bone loss (POI)
Thyroid dysfunction can independently accelerate bone turnover
Normal stimulated cortisol — Cushing's unlikely; do not stop the work-up here
Multi-hit model common: POI + thyroid + vitamin D deficiency may coexist
finalDiagnosis: Secondary osteoporosis in a 33-year-old woman — premature ovarian insufficiency (early menopause) as the primary driver, with contributory thyroid dysfunction to be characterised and corrected; Cushing syndrome excluded by normal stimulated cortisol. Plan: complete secondary osteoporosis panel (PTH, calcium, vitamin D, celiac, SPEP, medication audit); confirm POI (FSH, estradiol, karyotype, autoimmune ovarian antibodies); optimise thyroid (avoid suppressive/over-replacement LT4); initiate estrogen replacement until average menopause age if POI confirmed; add bone-specific therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab if HRT contraindicated or BMD severely low); calcium 1000–1200 mg + vitamin D repletion; repeat DXA in 1–2 years.
learningObjectives: Apply a structured secondary osteoporosis algorithm in a premenopausal/early menopausal young woman.
Characterise early menopause as POI and execute the POI diagnostic panel.
Interpret thyroid dysfunction in the bone context — hyperthyroidism and over-replacement LT4.
Understand what normal stimulated cortisol excludes — and what it does not.
Sequence treatment: underlying cause + HRT (POI) + bone-specific therapy + calcium/vitamin D.

items.title: How to frame Vimla's case — secondary osteoporosis at age 33 is never idiopathic
items.content: Osteoporosis in a 33-year-old woman is, by definition, secondary until a thorough work-up proves otherwise. Vimla's DXA shows low bone mineral density (confirm T-score ≤−2.5 at lumbar spine, femoral neck, or total hip — or osteopenia with fragility fracture). She carries two established risk factors — early menopause and thyroid dysfunction — and one important negative finding: normal stimulated cortisol, which makes Cushing syndrome unlikely. The fellow's task is not to stop at the first plausible explanation (estrogen deficiency) but to complete the exclusion algorithm, quantify each contributor, and build a treatment plan that addresses estrogen replacement (if POI), thyroid optimisation, and bone-specific pharmacotherapy together.
keyPoints: Osteoporosis at 33y is always secondary — full work-up mandatory.
Early menopause + thyroid dysfunction + normal cortisol — start here, don't stop here.
Treat underlying cause(s) + bone-specific therapy together.

items.title: Why DXA findings must be confirmed and contextualised before the work-up
items.content: Confirm the DXA report: lowest T-score site, Z-score (compare to age-matched peers — Z ≤−2.0 in a premenopausal/young woman is abnormal even if T-score has not crossed −2.5), and whether a fragility fracture (vertebral, wrist, hip) has occurred. In young women, the WHO T-score threshold (≤−2.5) defines osteoporosis, but Z-score ≤−2.0 also flags significant low BMD for age. FRAX may underestimate risk in secondary osteoporosis — do not rely on FRAX alone to withhold treatment in POI. Document height loss (>2 cm may indicate vertebral compression — order lateral spine X-ray or VFA if not done). Baseline photography of risk factors frames the urgency: a 33-year-old with POI loses trabecular bone rapidly in the first years after estrogen withdrawal.
keyPoints: Confirm T-score site; Z-score ≤−2.0 abnormal in young women.
FRAX underestimates secondary osteoporosis — don't use alone to deny treatment.
Height loss → vertebral imaging; POI causes rapid trabecular loss.

items.title: Why early menopause is the leading cause of secondary osteoporosis in Vimla
items.content: Estrogen deficiency accelerates bone remodelling: osteoclast activity exceeds osteoblast activity, with preferential loss of trabecular bone (spine, wrist). Menopause before age 40 defines premature ovarian insufficiency (POI); between 40 and 45 is early menopause — both confer significant fracture risk. The longer the estrogen-deficient interval, the lower the peak bone mass achieved and the greater cumulative loss. In a 33-year-old who has already reached menopause, she may have lost bone for years. Confirm: age at last menstrual period, history of chemotherapy, surgery, or autoimmune disease; FSH (>25–30 IU/L on two occasions ≥4 weeks apart in a woman >4 months amenorrheic), estradiol (low), LH; pelvic ultrasound (small ovaries, few follicles). POI is a diagnosis of exclusion — complete the secondary panel before attributing all bone loss to estrogen alone.
keyPoints: Estrogen deficiency → trabecular bone loss; POI = menopause <40y.
Confirm: amenorrhea duration, FSH ×2, estradiol, pelvic US.
POI is leading cause here but diagnosis of exclusion for bone loss.

items.title: How to complete the premature ovarian insufficiency diagnostic panel
items.content: Once POI is suspected: (1) Confirm hypergonadotropic hypogonadism — FSH elevated on two samples ≥4 weeks apart, low estradiol. (2) Karyotype (46,XX vs 45,X/46,XX mosaic Turner vs 46,XY gonadal dysgenesis). (3) Fragile X premutation (FMR1 CGG repeats — accounts for ~6% of POI). (4) Autoimmune panel: 21-hydroxylase/adrenal, thyroid peroxidase (TPO), transglutaminase (celiac — also causes bone loss), anti-ovarian antibodies. (5) History: chemotherapy, radiation, surgery, galactosemia, smoking. (6) Prolactin (exclude prolactinoma — cr-058). (7) Pituitary MRI only if hypogonadotropic pattern (low FSH) — not if FSH is high. POI requires estrogen replacement until average menopause age (~51 years) for bone, cardiovascular, and cognitive protection — unless contraindicated.
keyPoints: FSH ×2, karyotype, FMR1 premutation, autoimmune screen (TPO, adrenal, celiac, ovarian Abs).
Exclude prolactinoma; MRI if hypogonadotropic not hypergonadotropic.
HRT until ~51y unless contraindicated — bone + cardiovascular protection.

items.title: How thyroid dysfunction contributes to osteoporosis — and what to check in Vimla
items.content: Thyroid hormone excess — overt hyperthyroidism, subclinical hyperthyroidism (low TSH, normal high T4), or over-replacement with levothyroxine (TSH suppressed below target) — increases bone turnover and causes net bone loss, particularly trabecular bone. Hypothyroidism itself causes bone changes but less commonly severe osteoporosis; however, iatrogenic hyperthyroidism from excessive LT4 is a frequent contributor in treated patients. For Vimla: document current TSH, free T4, thyroid diagnosis (Graves, Hashimoto, post-ablation), LT4 dose, and whether TSH is inappropriately suppressed. Target TSH for bone safety in a non-thyroid-cancer patient is typically 0.5–2.5 mIU/L — not suppressed. If hyperthyroid, treat the hyperthyroidism (antithyroid drugs, ablation, or reduce LT4). Thyroid optimisation alone will not reverse POI-related bone loss but removes a co-contributor.
keyPoints: Thyroid excess (hyperthyroid or over-replaced LT4) → increased bone turnover.
Check TSH, free T4, LT4 dose; target TSH ~0.5–2.5 unless cancer indication.
Optimise thyroid — removes co-contributor but does not replace estrogen in POI.

items.title: What normal stimulated cortisol excludes — and why the work-up continues
items.content: A normal short Synacthen test (or equivalent stimulated cortisol response) effectively excludes adrenal insufficiency and makes Cushing syndrome unlikely in a patient without strong clinical stigmata (central obesity, striae, proximal weakness, easy bruising). This is an important negative finding — glucocorticoid excess is a common cause of secondary osteoporosis and must be excluded. However, normal stimulated cortisol does NOT exclude: POI, hyperthyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, celiac disease, myeloma, or medication-induced bone loss. Rare caveats: cyclic Cushing, mild autonomous cortisol secretion (MACS) with normal Synacthen — consider late-night salivary cortisol or 24-hour urinary free cortisol only if clinical suspicion persists. For Vimla, accept Cushing's as excluded and proceed with the remaining panel.
keyPoints: Normal Synacthen → Cushing's unlikely; important negative finding.
Does NOT exclude POI, thyroid, PHPT, celiac, myeloma, drugs.
If Cushing stigmata persist → 24h UFC or late-night salivary cortisol.

items.title: How to run the secondary osteoporosis exclusion panel in a young woman
items.content: Tier 1 (same visit): calcium (albumin-corrected), phosphate, ALP, 25-OH vitamin D, intact PTH, TSH, free T4, FSH, LH, estradiol, prolactin, celiac serology (tTG IgA + total IgA), CBC, creatinine/eGFR, LFTs. Tier 2: SPEP with immunofixation and serum free light chains (myeloma); 24-hour urinary calcium (hypercalciuria); testosterone, DHEAS if hyperandrogenic features; inflammatory markers (ESR, CRP) if inflammatory disease suspected. Tier 3 (by indication): karyotype, FMR1, autoimmune ovarian/adrenal antibodies; bone turnover markers (PINP, CTX — optional, for monitoring); transiliac bone biopsy only if diagnosis remains unclear after full panel. Medication audit: glucocorticoids (any route), PPIs, SSRIs, antiepileptics, heparin, GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, excess LT4, thiazolidinediones. Nutritional: calcium intake, eating disorder history, BMI.
keyPoints: Tier 1: Ca, PTH, vitamin D, thyroid, gonadotropins, celiac, renal, LFTs.
Tier 2: SPEP/FLC, 24h urine calcium, inflammatory markers.
Tier 3: karyotype, FMR1, antibodies; medication and nutritional audit.

items.title: Why primary hyperparathyroidism must be excluded even with normal cortisol
items.content: Primary hyperparathyroidism (PHPT) causes bone loss through increased bone turnover and cortical thinning — independent of cortisol status. Screen with albumin-corrected calcium and intact PTH: inappropriately normal or elevated PTH with high-normal or elevated calcium suggests PHPT (see cr-025). PHPT can coexist with POI and thyroid disease. If calcium is elevated, add 24-hour urinary calcium and calculate Ca/Cr clearance ratio to exclude FHH before referral for parathyroid surgery. PHPT in a 33-year-old triggers hereditary syndrome evaluation (MEN1, RET, CDC73). Do not attribute low BMD entirely to POI if PHPT is present — parathyroidectomy may partially restore bone density.
keyPoints: PHPT independent of cortisol — screen Ca + PTH in every case.
Elevated Ca + non-suppressed PTH → PHPT work-up (cr-025).
PHPT can coexist with POI — treat both.

items.title: Why celiac disease is a mandatory screen in young osteoporosis
items.content: Celiac disease causes osteoporosis through malabsorption of calcium and vitamin D, chronic inflammation, and sometimes secondary hyperparathyroidism — and may present with isolated low BMD without GI symptoms. tTG IgA with total IgA (exclude IgA deficiency) is the first-line screen; duodenal biopsy if serology positive. Celiac can coexist with autoimmune POI and autoimmune thyroiditis (polyglandular autoimmune syndrome). Treat with strict gluten-free diet — BMD may improve. Also assess vitamin D level (target >30 ng/mL) and dietary calcium intake (1000–1200 mg/day). Malabsorption from bariatric surgery, chronic pancreatitis, or eating disorders should be considered from history.
keyPoints: Celiac → malabsorption osteoporosis; screen tTG IgA even without GI symptoms.
Coexists with autoimmune POI/thyroiditis.
Gluten-free diet + vitamin D/calcium repletion.

items.title: Why Vimla likely has multiple concurrent contributors — not a single cause
items.content: Young women with secondary osteoporosis often have a 'multi-hit' model: POI (estrogen deficiency) + subclinical hyperthyroidism or over-replaced LT4 + vitamin D deficiency + low dietary calcium + prior glucocorticoid exposure. Each alone may not fully explain the DXA; together they produce substantial bone loss. The work-up quantifies each hit; treatment addresses all modifiable contributors. Do not stop the algorithm when POI is confirmed — thyroid may still need adjustment, vitamin D may be deficient, and PHPT may coexist. Reassess DXA after 1–2 years of optimised therapy to gauge response.
keyPoints: Multi-hit common: POI + thyroid + vitamin D + drugs + malabsorption.
Confirm POI but continue full panel — don't premature closure.
Repeat DXA 1–2 years after optimised treatment.

items.title: Why estrogen replacement is first-line for POI-related osteoporosis until age ~51
items.content: For confirmed POI, systemic estrogen replacement (with progestin if uterus intact) until the average age of natural menopause (~51 years) is recommended by international guidelines for bone protection, cardiovascular health, and symptom control — unless contraindicated (breast cancer, thromboembolism, active liver disease). Transdermal estradiol is preferred over oral in many protocols (lower thrombotic risk). HRT in POI is replacement, not the same risk profile as HRT started after age 60. Combined oral contraceptives are less ideal for POI (lower estrogen doses, not optimised for bone). If HRT is contraindicated, use bone-specific therapy (bisphosphonate or denosumab) — but HRT remains first-line when safe.
keyPoints: POI → systemic estrogen + progestin until ~51y unless contraindicated.
Transdermal estradiol preferred; COCs less ideal for bone.
HRT is replacement in POI — different risk profile than late menopause HRT.

items.title: When to add bisphosphonate, denosumab, or teriparatide in young secondary osteoporosis
items.content: Bone-specific therapy is added when: (1) HRT is contraindicated; (2) BMD is severely low (T-score ≤−2.5 or fragility fracture) despite HRT; or (3) rapid bone loss on serial DXA. Options: oral or IV bisphosphonate (alendronate, zoledronic acid — consider drug holiday after 3–5 years in younger patients due to long skeletal retention), denosumab (reversible — must not stop without follow-on antiresorptive), teriparatide (anabolic — reserved for severe osteoporosis or multiple fractures, limited duration 24 months). In POI with HRT started early, BMD often stabilises or improves — bisphosphonate may not be needed if HRT is tolerated. Calcium 1000–1200 mg/day and vitamin D to target >30 ng/mL are baseline for all patients. Weight-bearing and resistance exercise.
keyPoints: Bisphosphonate/denosumab if HRT contraindicated or severe BMD/fracture.
Teriparatide for severe/multiple fractures.
Calcium + vitamin D + exercise for all; HRT may suffice in POI if started early.

items.title: How to sequence Vimla's treatment plan
items.content: Step 1 — Complete diagnostic panel (Tier 1–2 above); confirm POI; characterise thyroid (adjust LT4 to target TSH 0.5–2.5); replete vitamin D and ensure adequate calcium. Step 2 — Start transdermal estrogen + progestin (if POI confirmed and no contraindication) — this is the primary bone-protective intervention. Step 3 — If T-score ≤−2.5 or fragility fracture despite HRT at 12 months, or HRT contraindicated, add bisphosphonate or denosumab. Step 4 — Repeat DXA at 12–24 months; bone turnover markers optional. Step 5 — Long-term POI surveillance: cardiovascular risk, autoimmune monitoring, fertility counselling if desired, psychological support. Document shared decision-making on HRT risks and benefits in a young woman.
keyPoints: Diagnose → replete vitamin D/calcium → optimise thyroid → start HRT (POI).
Add bisphosphonate/denosumab if severe or HRT contraindicated.
DXA 12–24 months; lifelong POI surveillance.

items.title: The stepwise navigation pathway for Vimla's secondary osteoporosis
items.content: Confirm DXA and fracture history → Early menopause panel (FSH, estradiol, karyotype, FMR1, autoimmune) → Thyroid panel (TSH, free T4, adjust LT4) → Normal cortisol accepted (Cushing excluded) → Exclusion panel (Ca, PTH, vitamin D, celiac, SPEP, medication audit) → Multi-hit integration → Treat: HRT for POI + thyroid optimisation + calcium/vitamin D ± bone-specific agent → Repeat DXA 1–2 years. The fellow must not anchor on POI alone or on normal cortisol alone — both are waypoints in a complete algorithm.
keyPoints: DXA → POI panel → thyroid → cortisol (done) → exclusion panel → treat all contributors.
HRT central for POI; thyroid optimise; bone agent if needed.
Don't anchor on single finding — complete the algorithm.

items.title: Secondary osteoporosis young woman — diagnostic checklist
items.content: ☐ Confirm DXA T-score and Z-score; vertebral imaging if height loss. ☐ POI: FSH ×2, estradiol, amenorrhea history, karyotype, FMR1, TPO/adrenal/ovarian Abs. ☐ Thyroid: TSH, free T4, LT4 dose — target TSH 0.5–2.5. ☐ Cortisol: stimulated test normal — Cushing excluded. ☐ Ca, albumin, PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, phosphate, ALP. ☐ Celiac: tTG IgA + total IgA. ☐ SPEP + free light chains. ☐ Prolactin. ☐ Medication audit (steroids, PPI, LT4 excess, etc.). ☐ BMI, calcium intake, eating disorder screen. ☐ Treat: HRT (POI) + thyroid optimise + Ca/Vit D ± bisphosphonate/denosumab. ☐ DXA repeat 12–24 months.
keyPoints: POI + thyroid + exclusion panel despite normal cortisol.
HRT first-line POI; bone agent if severe/contraindicated.
Repeat DXA 12–24 months.

items.question: Why is early menopause the leading explanation for osteoporosis in a 33-year-old woman?
items.answer: Because estrogen deficiency removes the brake on osteoclast-mediated bone resorption, causing rapid trabecular bone loss — most pronounced in the first years after menopause. A 33-year-old with early menopause may have been estrogen-deficient for years, preventing attainment of peak bone mass and accelerating decline. POI is the commonest endocrine cause of secondary osteoporosis in young women, though the full exclusion panel must still be completed.
keyPoints: Estrogen deficiency → unopposed bone resorption.
Early menopause → years of deficient estrogen at young age.
Leading cause but not the only cause — complete work-up.

items.question: How should a fellow navigate Vimla's case given early menopause, thyroid dysfunction, and normal stimulated cortisol?
items.answer: Treat normal cortisol as excluding Cushing's, then: (1) Confirm POI with FSH ×2, estradiol, karyotype, FMR1, autoimmune panel. (2) Characterise thyroid — if TSH suppressed or hyperthyroid, optimise (reduce LT4 or treat hyperthyroidism). (3) Complete exclusion panel: Ca/PTH, vitamin D, celiac, SPEP, prolactin, medication audit. (4) Start transdermal estrogen + progestin if POI confirmed (until ~51y). (5) Replete calcium and vitamin D. (6) Add bisphosphonate or denosumab if HRT contraindicated or BMD severely low. (7) Repeat DXA in 12–24 months.
keyPoints: Cushing excluded → POI panel + thyroid optimise + full exclusion.
HRT first-line POI; bone agent if needed.
DXA follow-up 12–24 months.

items.stem: Vimla has normal stimulated cortisol on short Synacthen testing. What is the most appropriate inference?
options: Cushing syndrome is unlikely — proceed with the remaining secondary osteoporosis work-up
Cushing syndrome is definitively excluded forever — no further testing needed
Adrenal insufficiency is confirmed
Osteoporosis is therefore idiopathic
items.explanation: Normal stimulated cortisol makes Cushing's unlikely and allows the work-up to focus on POI, thyroid, PHPT, celiac, and other causes. It does not exclude all cortisol-related pathology if clinical suspicion is high, and does not make osteoporosis idiopathic.

items.stem: POI is confirmed in this 33-year-old. What is the first-line treatment for bone protection?
options: Systemic estrogen plus progestin until approximately age 51 unless contraindicated
Bisphosphonate alone without estrogen
Calcium and vitamin D only — no hormone therapy in young women
Observation until age 50 when menopause treatment normally begins
items.explanation: POI guidelines recommend estrogen replacement (with progestin if uterus intact) until average menopause age for bone, cardiovascular, and symptom benefits. Bisphosphonate is second-line if HRT contraindicated. Calcium/vitamin D alone insufficient; waiting until 50 denies years of protection.

items.stem: She takes levothyroxine for hypothyroidism. TSH is 0.08 mIU/L with normal free T4. How does this affect her bones?
options: Suppressed TSH from over-replacement causes increased bone turnover and contributes to bone loss — reduce LT4 dose
Suppressed TSH protects bone density
TSH level is irrelevant to bone metabolism
She should stop levothyroxine entirely
items.explanation: Iatrogenic subclinical hyperthyroidism from excessive LT4 increases bone turnover and causes bone loss, especially trabecular bone. TSH should be adjusted to approximately 0.5–2.5 mIU/L in non-cancer patients. Stopping LT4 entirely would cause hypothyroidism.

items.stem: Which investigation is mandatory in every young woman with secondary osteoporosis even if POI is already known?
options: Albumin-corrected calcium and intact PTH to exclude hyperparathyroidism
Short Synacthen test — even if already normal
Bone marrow biopsy
Karyotype — only if FSH is normal
items.explanation: PHPT must be screened in every osteoporosis case with calcium and PTH — it can coexist with POI. Synacthen is already done; bone marrow biopsy is not routine; karyotype is indicated in POI with elevated FSH.

items.stem: Which test identifies fragile X premutation as a cause of POI?
options: FMR1 CGG repeat analysis
Standard 46,XX karyotype alone
AMH level only
Prolactin level
items.explanation: FMR1 CGG premutation accounts for ~6% of POI and requires specific repeat analysis — not detected on standard karyotype. AMH is useful for ovarian reserve but does not diagnose fragile X. Prolactin excludes prolactinoma.

items.stem: HRT is contraindicated in this patient due to prior breast cancer. T-score is −2.8 at lumbar spine. What is the most appropriate bone-specific therapy?
options: Bisphosphonate (e.g. alendronate or zoledronic acid) or denosumab with calcium and vitamin D
Estrogen replacement despite contraindication
Observation only — no pharmacotherapy in young women
Teriparatide as first-line for all POI patients
items.explanation: When HRT is contraindicated, bisphosphonate or denosumab with calcium/vitamin D is appropriate for T-score −2.8. Estrogen is contraindicated. Observation ignores severe BMD. Teriparatide is reserved for severe/multiple fractures, not first-line.

items.statement: Osteoporosis in a 33-year-old woman should be treated as idiopathic unless a secondary cause is identified.
items.explanation: False. Osteoporosis in a young woman is secondary until proven otherwise — a full exclusion work-up is mandatory.

items.statement: Normal stimulated cortisol excludes the need for calcium and PTH measurement.
items.explanation: False. Normal cortisol excludes Cushing's but PHPT screening (calcium, PTH) is mandatory independently in all secondary osteoporosis work-ups.

items.statement: POI and thyroid dysfunction can coexist as concurrent contributors to bone loss in the same patient.
items.explanation: True. Multi-hit model is common — estrogen deficiency and thyroid hormone excess both increase bone turnover and can coexist, requiring treatment of both.

items.assertion: Estrogen replacement should be offered to Vimla if POI is confirmed and there are no contraindications.
items.reason: Estrogen deficiency from POI accelerates trabecular bone loss and increases fracture risk in young women.
items.explanation: Both true — POI causes bone loss through estrogen deficiency; replacement until ~51y is guideline-recommended. Option 0.

items.assertion: Normal stimulated cortisol means no further endocrine testing is required for Vimla's osteoporosis.
items.reason: POI, hyperthyroidism, hyperparathyroidism, and celiac disease cause secondary osteoporosis independently of cortisol status.
items.explanation: Assertion false (full panel still required) but reason true (multiple independent causes). Option 3.

items.assertion: Over-replacement with levothyroxine causing TSH suppression can contribute to bone loss.
items.reason: Excess thyroid hormone increases bone turnover and osteoclast activity.
items.explanation: Both true — iatrogenic subclinical hyperthyroidism from LT4 over-replacement increases bone turnover. Option 0.

items.question: List six investigations in the Tier 1 secondary osteoporosis panel for Vimla.
items.answer: Albumin-corrected calcium, intact PTH, 25-OH vitamin D, TSH and free T4, FSH and estradiol (POI confirmation), celiac serology (tTG IgA with total IgA), creatinine/eGFR, and prolactin. (Any six from this Tier 1 list accepted.)

items.question: Name three tests specific to the POI diagnostic panel beyond FSH and estradiol.
items.answer: (1) Karyotype (Turner syndrome, mosaicism). (2) FMR1 CGG repeat analysis (fragile X premutation). (3) Autoimmune antibodies (TPO, 21-hydroxylase/adrenal, anti-ovarian, transglutaminase for celiac).

items.question: In what order should treatment be initiated once POI and subclinical hyperthyroidism from LT4 over-replacement are confirmed?
items.answer: (1) Reduce levothyroxine to target TSH 0.5–2.5 mIU/L. (2) Replete vitamin D (target >30 ng/mL) and ensure calcium intake 1000–1200 mg/day. (3) Start transdermal estrogen + progestin for POI (until ~51y if tolerated). (4) Add bisphosphonate or denosumab only if HRT contraindicated or BMD remains severely low at follow-up. (5) Repeat DXA in 12–24 months.
